conflicts.title: Type performance vs correct type inference

decision_a.title: Replace type intersection with union in Hono's route method to reduce type instantiation cost
decision_a.position: Type performance is a top priority and must be protected even when changing handler type semantics.
decision_a.quote: Plus, regarding the types, we must care about the performance, most importantly.

decision_b.title: Propagate middleware response types through RPC by extending MiddlewareHandler with a fourth type parameter
decision_b.position: Correct middleware type inference is worth accepting a significant TypeScript instantiation increase.
decision_b.quote: I think we can accept this PR, though bringing the performance degradation since making consistency is significant, and we may resolve the performance issue in other ways.

conflicts.contradiction: Decision A declares type performance the paramount concern and accepts correctness compromises (changing handler types, breaking downstream generators) to get it; Decision B reverses the ranking, accepting an 18.6% instantiation increase because correctness outweighs performance. The same repo now has two contradictory priorities guiding which side of the tradeoff wins.
conflicts.resolution_hint: A later decision should state explicitly whether correctness or compile-time performance takes precedence, or define a per-feature budget for instantiation growth.

conflicts.title: Contentless status codes: type ban vs runtime override

decision_a.title: Restrict c.json/c.text/c.html to ContentfulStatusCode at the type level
decision_a.position: c.json/c.text/c.html must reject 101/204/205/304 at compile time to prevent runtime TypeErrors.
decision_a.quote: All in all, prohibits returning `c.json()` or `c.text()` or `c.html()` with 101, 204, 205, and 304 status codes.

decision_b.title: Add fast path to c.json() matching c.text() optimization to skip #newResponse() and Headers allocation
decision_b.position: c.json() fast path constructs a Response directly without status or headers checks when arg/headers are absent.
decision_b.quote: When all conditions are met (the common case for simple JSON responses), it creates the Response directly with an inline header object instead of going through `#newResponse()`

conflicts.contradiction: Decision A's guarantee rests on ContentfulStatusCode being enforced through the normal response path. Decision B's fast path short-circuits around that path entirely when no explicit status is given, so any code that sets a contentless status through alternative channels (e.g., prepared headers bypassed, middleware-set status, or future callers) would silently bypass the invariant A promised. The guard is type-level; the fast path is runtime-level and assumes type-level enforcement is sufficient.
conflicts.resolution_hint: Document that the fast path depends on the ContentfulStatusCode type guard and add a runtime assertion in development mode, or narrow the fast-path condition to exclude any code that could set a contentless status.

conflicts.title: Raw Request preservation vs hot-path performance

decision_a.title: Expose request cloning as a standalone `cloneRawRequest` utility rather than modifying HonoRequest
decision_a.position: HonoRequest must remain a simple, small core object; body-cloning logic should not live in its hot path.
decision_a.quote: But it's not good that the logic/code has increased. The core object, `HonoRequest`, should be simple and small.

decision_b.title: Propagate middleware response types through RPC by extending MiddlewareHandler with a fourth type parameter
decision_b.position: Growing core type machinery (and its perf cost) is acceptable when behavior consistency demands it.
decision_b.quote: it is also important that the type inference works as expected correctly

conflicts.contradiction: Decision A refuses to grow HonoRequest even for a clearly requested correctness feature, citing minimalism as non-negotiable. Decision B grows the core middleware type signature (adding a 4th type parameter affecting every handler) for a similar correctness need. The two decisions apply opposite minimalism thresholds to comparable 'core must stay small' arguments.
conflicts.resolution_hint: Articulate a rule for when core surface may grow (e.g., 'types may grow for correctness but runtime paths may not'), so the distinction between the two cases is explicit rather than taste-based.

conflicts.title: Node-specific APIs in core

decision_a.title: Accept Uint8Array (not node:buffer Buffer) in c.body() to support binary responses
decision_a.position: The core of hono must not depend on node:* modules because they are not Web Standards.
decision_a.quote: It is importing just types, but I think we should not use `node:*` modules in the core of `hono`. Actually, `Buffer` is not in Web Standards API.

decision_b.title: Use `join` for serve-static path resolution to fix Windows drive-letter loss and path-traversal via backslash
decision_b.position: Runtime-specific serve-static adapters should use node:path's join to handle platform paths correctly.
decision_b.quote: For Deno, as you said, we can use `node:path`'s `join`. Updated

conflicts.contradiction: Decision A established that node:* modules (even as types) don't belong in hono's core because Buffer isn't Web Standard. Decision B then introduces node:path into the Deno serve-static adapter on the grounds that it is 'built into Deno,' applying the opposite reasoning to another node:* module that is equally not Web Standard. The policy of 'no node:* in core' is being quietly relaxed on a per-module basis without an explicit carve-out.
conflicts.resolution_hint: Define which node:* modules are permitted in which adapter boundaries (e.g., allow node:path in runtime-specific adapters but keep Web-Standards-only in src/*), rather than deciding case-by-case.
